question: According to the octet rule, atoms tend to form bonds so that each atom achieves:
explanation: The octet rule states atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve 8 electrons in their outermost shell, resembling the electron configuration of the nearest noble gas. This applies mainly to second-period elements (C, N, O, F). Exceptions: (1) Incomplete octets â€” BFâ‚ƒ (6 electrons on B), BeClâ‚‚ (4 on Be); (2) Expanded octets â€” PClâ‚… (10 on P), SFâ‚† (12 on S) using available 3d orbitals; (3) Odd-electron molecules â€” NO, NOâ‚‚ (unpaired electron). Hydrogen follows the duet rule (2 electrons, like helium).

question: A purely ionic bond forms between elements with:
options: Low ionization energies in both elements
explanation: Ionic bonds form when there is a large electronegativity difference between atoms (generally خ”EN > 1.7 on the Pauling scale), causing electron transfer rather than sharing. The more electronegative element gains electrons (anion); the less electronegative loses them (cation). Example: NaCl: خ”EN = 3.0 âˆ’ 0.9 = 2.1 (ionic). For خ”EN â‰ˆ 0: pure covalent (Hâ‚‚, Clâ‚‚). For 0 < خ”EN < 1.7: polar covalent (HCl, Hâ‚‚O). The boundary is approximate â€” 50% ionic character occurs around خ”EN â‰ˆ 1.7. Also required: low ionization energy for the metal and high electron affinity for the non-metal.

question: The bond angle in water (Hâ‚‚O) is approximately 104.5آ°. This is less than the tetrahedral angle (109.5آ°) because:
options: Oxygen is more electronegative than hydrogen
The two lone pairs on oxygen exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing the H-O-H angle
Water molecule is linear, not tetrahedral
The H-O bond is longer than expected
explanation: VSEPR theory: electron pairs arrange to minimize repulsion. In Hâ‚‚O: 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 4 electron pairs â†’ tetrahedral electron geometry. But lone pairs occupy more space (greater repulsion) than bonding pairs because: lone pairs are attracted to only one nucleus (spread out more) vs. bonding pairs attracted to two nuclei (more constrained). Repulsion order: lp-lp > lp-bp > bp-bp. Two lone pairs push the two O-H bonds closer together, reducing bond angle from 109.5آ° (pure tetrahedral) to 104.5آ°. NHâ‚ƒ (1 lone pair) has 107آ°, intermediate between Hâ‚‚O and CHâ‚„ (109.5آ°).

question: Which of the following molecules has a linear geometry?
explanation: COâ‚‚ (O=C=O) is linear (180آ° bond angle): C has 2 double bonds and 0 lone pairs â†’ 2 bonding regions â†’ linear. Hâ‚‚O: 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs = bent (104.5آ°). NHâ‚ƒ: 3 bonding + 1 lone pair = trigonal pyramidal (107آ°). SOâ‚‚: S has 2 bonding groups + 1 lone pair = bent (119آ°). VSEPR: count total electron pairs (or electron domains) around central atom. Linear = 2 electron domains, no lone pairs. Examples: BeHâ‚‚ (sp), COâ‚‚ (sp), HCN (sp), BeClâ‚‚ (sp). Key test: linear requires exactly 2 electron domains with 0 lone pairs on central atom.

question: The hybridization of carbon in ethyne (Câ‚‚Hâ‚‚) is:
explanation: Ethyne (HCâ‰،CH): each carbon forms 2 sigma bonds (one C-H, one C-C) and is part of a triple bond (1دƒ + 2د€). For 2 sigma bonds around each C: 2 hybrid orbitals needed â†’ sp hybridization (1 s + 1 p orbital hybridize, leaving 2 unhybridized p orbitals for 2 د€ bonds). The two sp hybrid orbitals are at 180آ° (linear geometry). The remaining 2 p orbitals on each C form 2 د€ bonds with the adjacent C. Result: HCâ‰،CH is linear with a triple bond. sp hybridized C has linear geometry (180آ° angle); spآ² is trigonal planar (120آ°); spآ³ is tetrahedral (109.5آ°).

question: According to Valence Bond Theory, a sigma (دƒ) bond forms by:
options: Side-by-side (lateral) overlap of two p orbitals
Head-on (end-to-end) overlap of orbitals along the bond axis
Overlap of s and p orbitals at right angles to bond axis
Delocalization of electrons across multiple atoms
explanation: Sigma (دƒ) bonds form by head-on (axial, end-to-end) overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis. This direct overlap results in maximum electron density along the bond axis. Examples: s-s (H-H in Hâ‚‚), s-p (H-Cl), p-p head-on (Cl-Cl in Clâ‚‚), spآ³-spآ³ (C-C in ethane). Pi (د€) bonds form by lateral (side-to-side) overlap of unhybridized p orbitals perpendicular to the bond axis, with electron density above and below the axis. د€ bonds are weaker than دƒ bonds because lateral overlap is less efficient. Every single bond is one دƒ bond; double bond = 1دƒ + 1د€; triple bond = 1دƒ + 2د€.

question: The formal charge on nitrogen in the nitrate ion (NOâ‚ƒâپ») is:
explanation: In NOâ‚ƒâپ», resonance structures show N with one double bond to one O and single bonds to two Oâپ» atoms. Formal charge = (valence electrons) âˆ’ (non-bonding electrons) âˆ’ آ½(bonding electrons). For N in NOâ‚ƒâپ»: Lewis structure with one N=O double bond (4 electrons shared) and two N-O single bonds (2 electrons each): Formal charge = 5 âˆ’ 0 âˆ’ آ½(8) = 5 âˆ’ 4 = +1. The two singly-bonded O atoms each have FC = âˆ’1, the doubly-bonded O has FC = 0. Net charge = +1 + 0 + (âˆ’1) + (âˆ’1) = âˆ’1 âœ“ (matches the ion's charge). This +1 formal charge on N in nitrate is a frequently tested calculation.

question: Which of the following molecules is polar?
explanation: A molecule is polar if it has polar bonds AND the dipole moments do NOT cancel due to molecular geometry. COâ‚‚: 2 C=O bonds (polar), linear symmetry â†’ dipoles cancel â†’ nonpolar. CClâ‚„: 4 C-Cl bonds (polar), tetrahedral symmetry â†’ dipoles cancel â†’ nonpolar. BFâ‚ƒ: 3 B-F bonds (polar), trigonal planar symmetry â†’ dipoles cancel â†’ nonpolar. CHClâ‚ƒ (chloroform): 1 C-H bond + 3 C-Cl bonds in tetrahedral geometry â†’ asymmetric distribution â†’ dipoles DO NOT cancel â†’ POLAR molecule. CHClâ‚ƒ has a net dipole moment (~1.87 D) and is used as a solvent. The C-H and C-Cl dipoles do not cancel because their directions and magnitudes differ.

question: The bond order of Oâ‚‚ molecule according to Molecular Orbital Theory is:
explanation: MO electron configuration of Oâ‚‚ (16 electrons): دƒ1sآ² دƒ*1sآ² دƒ2sآ² دƒ*2sآ² دƒ2pآ² د€2pâپ´ د€*2pآ². Bond order = (bonding electrons âˆ’ antibonding electrons)/2 = (8 âˆ’ 4)/2... wait, let me recount: دƒ1sآ²(2) دƒ*1sآ²(2) دƒ2sآ²(2) دƒ*2sآ²(2) دƒ2pzآ²(2) د€2pxآ²د€2pyآ²(4) د€*2pxآ¹د€*2pyآ¹(2) = bonding: 2+2+2+4=10, antibonding: 2+2+2=6. BO = (10âˆ’6)/2 = 2. Bond order = 2, consistent with O=O double bond. Also, the 2 unpaired electrons in the degenerate د€* orbitals explain Oâ‚‚'s paramagnetism â€” a major triumph of MO theory over Lewis theory (which predicts a diamagnetic double bond).

question:
Assertion (A): The bond length of Nâ‚‚ is less than that of Nâ‚‚âپ؛.
Reason (R): Nâ‚‚âپ؛ has one fewer electron than Nâ‚‚, resulting in a lower bond order.
options: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A
Both A and R are true, but R does not explain A
A is true, R is false
A is false, R is true
explanation: Nâ‚‚ MO config (14e): دƒ1sآ² دƒ*1sآ² دƒ2sآ² دƒ*2sآ² د€2pâپ´ دƒ2pآ². Bonding = 8, antibonding = 4. BO(Nâ‚‚) = (8âˆ’4)/2 = 2... actually for Nâ‚‚: bonding MOs: دƒ1s, دƒ2s, دƒ2p, د€2pأ—2 = 8e bonding; antibonding: دƒ*1s, دƒ*2s = 4e antibonding. BO = (8âˆ’4)/2 = 2? No â€” correct Nâ‚‚ BO: (10âˆ’4)/2 = 3. Nâ‚‚ has BO=3. Nâ‚‚âپ؛ has 13 electrons â€” one electron removed from a bonding د€2p MO: BO(Nâ‚‚âپ؛) = (9âˆ’4)/2 = 2.5. Since BO(Nâ‚‚)=3 > BO(Nâ‚‚âپ؛)=2.5: Nâ‚‚ has stronger, shorter bond. Assertion A is TRUE (Nâ‚‚ shorter). Reason R is TRUE (Nâ‚‚âپ؛ has lower BO). R correctly explains A.

question: Which of the following pairs of molecules are isoelectronic AND isostructural?
explanation: Isoelectronic = same number of electrons; isostructural = same shape/geometry. NOâ‚‚âپ» (nitrite): 18 electrons (7+8+8+1), bent with 1 lone pair on N. SOâ‚‚: 32 electrons... wait, SOâ‚‚ has 32 electrons â€” NOT isoelectronic with NOâ‚‚âپ» (18e). Let me reconsider: Nâ‚‚ and CO: both have 14 electrons, both are linear diatomics â€” isoelectronic AND isostructural âœ“. COâ‚‚ and SOâ‚‚: COâ‚‚ is linear, SOâ‚‚ is bent â€” NOT isostructural. BFâ‚ƒ is trigonal planar, NHâ‚ƒ is trigonal pyramidal â€” NOT isostructural. Option B (Nâ‚‚ and CO) is the best answer: both diatomic, 14 electrons, linear, triple bond character.

question: Hydrogen bonding is strongest in which of the following compounds?
explanation: Hydrogen bond strength depends on: (1) electronegativity of the atom bonded to H â€” higher electronegativity â†’ stronger H-bond; (2) size of the electronegative atom â€” smaller size â†’ greater charge density â†’ stronger H-bond. Electronegativity order: F(4.0) > O(3.5) > N(3.0) > Cl(3.0). F is most electronegative AND smallest â†’ HF forms the STRONGEST individual H-bonds. However, Hâ‚‚O has TWO lone pairs and forms an extended network, giving water its anomalously high boiling point. HCl: Cl is electronegative but large â†’ weak H-bonds. Strongest H-bond energy: HF (29 kJ/mol) > Hâ‚‚O (21 kJ/mol) > NHâ‚ƒ (13 kJ/mol).

question: The VSEPR geometry of XeFâ‚„ is:
explanation: XeFâ‚„: Xe has 8 valence electrons. 4 are used in 4 Xe-F bonds, leaving 4 non-bonding electrons = 2 lone pairs. Total electron pairs = 4 bp + 2 lp = 6 â†’ octahedral electron geometry. The 2 lone pairs occupy axial positions (to minimize lp-lp repulsion â€” placed at 180آ° to each other). The 4 bonding pairs occupy equatorial positions in a SQUARE PLANAR arrangement. Molecular shape = square planar (120آ° bond angles would be trigonal planar; square planar has 90آ° F-Xe-F angles). Other examples of square planar: IClâ‚„âپ», [PtClâ‚„]آ²âپ» (noble metal complexes). Xe compounds were first made in 1962 (Bartlett) â€” revolutionizing noble gas chemistry.

question: In BeClâ‚‚ molecule, the central atom Be has only 4 electrons in its valence shell after bonding. This makes BeClâ‚‚ an electron-deficient compound. What happens to the empty orbital in BeClâ‚‚?
options: BeClâ‚‚ forms a dimer (Beâ‚‚Clâ‚„) using chlorine as bridging atoms (3-center 2-electron bonds)
The empty orbital remains vacant and BeClâ‚‚ is stable as a monomer
BeClâ‚‚ accepts an electron pair from a donor molecule to complete its octet, acting as a Lewis acid
BeClâ‚‚ spontaneously ionizes to Beآ²âپ؛ and 2Clâپ»
explanation: BeClâ‚‚ is electron-deficient (6 electrons around Be, incomplete octet). The empty sp hybrid orbital makes Be a Lewis acid â€” it ACCEPTS electron pairs from Lewis bases (electron donors). Example: BeClâ‚‚ + 2:NHâ‚ƒ â†’ Clâ‚‚Be(NHâ‚ƒ)â‚‚ (tetrahedral adduct, Be now has 8 electrons). Similarly, AlClâ‚ƒ (6 electrons on Al) is a Lewis acid used in Friedel-Crafts reactions. BeClâ‚‚ does polymerize in solid state through Cl bridging (option A is partially correct for solid state), but its primary chemical behavior as a Lewis acid (option C) is the more fundamental and exam-relevant answer.

question: The hybridization of the central atom in PClâ‚… is:
explanation: PClâ‚…: P forms 5 bonds (5 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs). For 5 electron pairs: spآ³d hybridization (uses 1s + 3p + 1d orbitals = 5 hybrid orbitals). The geometry is trigonal bipyramidal: 3 equatorial bonds at 120آ° (equatorial plane) and 2 axial bonds at 90آ° to equatorial. Axial bonds are slightly longer/weaker than equatorial (more repulsion from equatorial). PClâ‚… is an exception to the octet rule â€” P uses 3d orbitals (n=3) to expand its valence shell. N (n=2) cannot form NClâ‚… (no 2d orbitals). spآ³dآ² gives octahedral (6 bonds like SFâ‚†). spآ³ gives tetrahedral (4 bonds). spآ²d gives square planar (exotic).

question: Resonance occurs in a molecule when:
options: The molecule has only one valid Lewis structure
Two or more Lewis structures can be drawn for the same molecule, differing only in electron pair arrangement (not atom positions)
The atoms in the molecule vibrate rapidly
The molecule contains only single bonds
explanation: Resonance: when a molecule cannot be accurately represented by a single Lewis structure, multiple resonance structures are drawn. In reality, the molecule is a resonance HYBRID â€” a weighted average of all contributing structures. The actual structure is more stable than any single resonance structure (resonance stabilization energy). Examples: benzene (6 equivalent structures), carbonate ion COâ‚ƒآ²âپ» (3 structures), nitrate NOâ‚ƒâپ» (3 structures), ozone Oâ‚ƒ (2 structures). Key rule: atom positions do NOT change between resonance structures â€” only electron pair arrangements differ. Resonance is NOT an oscillation or equilibrium between structures.

question: The bond order of NO molecule according to MO theory is:
explanation: NO has 11 electrons. MO filling: دƒ1sآ² دƒ*1sآ² دƒ2sآ² دƒ*2sآ² دƒ2pآ² د€2pâپ´ د€*2pآ¹. Bonding electrons: دƒ1s(2) + دƒ2s(2) + دƒ2p(2) + د€2p(4) = 10. Antibonding: دƒ*1s(2) + دƒ*2s(2) + د€*2p(1) = 5. Bond order = (10âˆ’5)/2 = 2.5. NO has bond order 2.5, between Nâ‚‚ (BO=3) and Oâ‚‚ (BO=2). The single unpaired electron in د€*2p makes NO paramagnetic and a radical. NO is a biological signaling molecule (vasodilation, neurotransmission) â€” its discovery as a physiological messenger earned the 1998 Nobel Prize in Physiology/Medicine.

question: Which of the following has the shortest carbon-carbon bond?
explanation: Carbon-carbon bond length decreases as bond order increases (more electrons between atoms means they're held closer together): C-C single bond (ethane): 154 pm, BO = 1. C=C double bond (ethene): 134 pm, BO = 2. Câ‰،C triple bond (ethyne): 120 pm, BO = 3. Benzene C-C bond: 139 pm, BO = 1.5 (intermediate between single and double, delocalized). Order of C-C bond lengths (shortest to longest): ethyne < benzene < ethene < ethane. Shorter bonds are STRONGER bonds: ethyne's triple bond (835 kJ/mol) > benzene > ethene > ethane (346 kJ/mol).

question: In MO theory, which molecular orbitals are lower in energy than the atomic orbitals they are formed from?
options: All MOs have the same energy as their parent atomic orbitals
explanation: When two atomic orbitals combine to form MOs: Bonding MO: formed by constructive interference (in-phase overlap) â€” has LOWER energy than the contributing AOs, with increased electron density between nuclei (stabilizing). Antibonding MO (*): formed by destructive interference (out-of-phase overlap) â€” has HIGHER energy than the contributing AOs, with a nodal plane between nuclei and decreased electron density between atoms (destabilizing). Non-bonding MOs have the same energy as AOs. Molecules are stable when more electrons are in bonding than antibonding MOs (positive bond order). Electrons in antibonding MOs destabilize the molecule.

question: The structure of ice (Hâ‚‚O in solid state) is a tetrahedral network where each water molecule forms 4 hydrogen bonds. This explains which anomalous property of ice?
options: Ice has higher density than liquid water
Ice has lower density than liquid water
Ice melts at an unusually low temperature
Ice does not dissolve ionic compounds
explanation: In ice, each Hâ‚‚O forms 4 H-bonds in a tetrahedral arrangement, creating a rigid, open crystal lattice with large hexagonal spaces. This open structure makes ice LESS dense (0.917 g/mL) than liquid water (1.000 g/mL). Upon melting, some H-bonds break, the rigid lattice collapses, molecules pack more efficiently â†’ liquid water is denser. This unusual density behavior (solid less dense than liquid) means ice floats on water. Ecological/biological significance: floating ice insulates water below, allowing aquatic life to survive in winter. Most substances have their solid form DENSER than liquid form.

question: The bond angle of NHâ‚ƒ (107آ°) is less than the tetrahedral angle (109.5آ°) but more than the H-O-H angle in water (104.5آ°). The correct explanation using VSEPR is:
options: N is less electronegative than O, so bonds are longer in NHâ‚ƒ
NHâ‚ƒ has 3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair; Hâ‚‚O has 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs; more lone pairs cause more compression of bond angles
Nitrogen uses spآ² hybridization while oxygen uses spآ³
The H-N-H angle depends only on the size of the hydrogen atoms
explanation: VSEPR logic: each lone pair exerts more repulsion than each bonding pair. NHâ‚ƒ: 3 bp + 1 lp â†’ 1 lone pair compresses bond angles from 109.5آ° to 107آ°. Hâ‚‚O: 2 bp + 2 lp â†’ 2 lone pairs cause greater compression, giving 104.5آ°. Trend: CHâ‚„ (0 lp, 109.5آ°) > NHâ‚ƒ (1 lp, 107آ°) > Hâ‚‚O (2 lp, 104.5آ°). Each additional lone pair reduces bond angle by ~2-3آ°. This is a fundamental VSEPR concept: lone pairâ€“lone pair repulsion > lone pairâ€“bond pair > bond pairâ€“bond pair repulsion. Both N and O are spآ³ hybridized in these molecules.

question: A coordinate (dative) covalent bond differs from a regular covalent bond in:
options: Strength â€” coordinate bonds are always weaker
Origin â€” both electrons come from the same atom (electron pair donor/Lewis base)
The type of orbitals involved â€” only p orbitals form coordinate bonds
Its direction â€” coordinate bonds are always perpendicular to other bonds
explanation: In a regular covalent bond, each atom contributes one electron to the shared pair. In a coordinate (dative) covalent bond, BOTH electrons come from the same atom (the Lewis base/electron pair donor). Example: BFâ‚ƒ + :NHâ‚ƒ â†’ Fâ‚ƒBâ†گNHâ‚ƒ â€” the nitrogen's lone pair fills the empty orbital of B. Once formed, a coordinate bond is IDENTICAL in strength and properties to a regular covalent bond â€” the distinction is only in origin. Other examples: Hâ‚ƒOâپ؛ (one O-H bond is coordinate), NHâ‚„âپ؛ (one N-H bond is coordinate), [Cu(NHâ‚ƒ)â‚„]آ²âپ؛ (all four Nâ†’Cu bonds). The arrow notation (â†’) indicates the bond is dative.

question: Which of the following molecules shows spآ² hybridization in the central atom?
explanation: spآ² hybridization: 1s + 2p â†’ 3 spآ² hybrid orbitals arranged at 120آ° (trigonal planar). Requires 3 electron domains (3 bonds + 0 lp, or 2 bonds + 1 lp for bent). BFâ‚ƒ: B has 3 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs â†’ spآ² â†’ trigonal planar âœ“. CHâ‚„: C has 4 bonds â†’ spآ³. Hâ‚‚O: O has 2 bonds + 2 lp = 4 domains â†’ spآ³. PClâ‚…: P has 5 bonds â†’ spآ³d. Other spآ² examples: C in CHâ‚‚=CHâ‚‚ (each C), C in benzene, N in pyridine, O in some compounds. spآ² hybridized atoms are always in the same plane as their bonded atoms, allowing adjacent unhybridized p orbitals to overlap and form د€ bonds.

question: The lattice energy of NaCl is much higher than that of NaI. The MAIN reason is:
options: Cl has higher electron affinity than I
The ionic radius of Clâپ» is smaller than Iâپ», resulting in shorter interionic distance and stronger electrostatic attraction
Naâپ؛ bonds more strongly to Clâپ» than to Iâپ» due to better orbital overlap
The molar mass of NaCl is less than NaI
explanation: Lattice energy (U) is the energy released when gaseous ions form a crystal lattice. By Coulomb's law: U âˆ‌ (qâ‚ٹ أ— qâ‚‹) / r, where r is the interionic distance. NaCl vs NaI: same charges (qâ‚ٹ = qâ‚‹ = 1 for both). The key difference is ionic radius: Clâپ» (181 pm) < Iâپ» (220 pm). Smaller Clâپ» â†’ shorter Naâپ؛-Clâپ» distance â†’ larger lattice energy. NaCl lattice energy â‰ˆ 787 kJ/mol vs NaI â‰ˆ 699 kJ/mol. This trend explains why fluorides generally have the highest lattice energies among halides of the same metal (Fâپ» is smallest at 133 pm).

question: The dipole moment of a molecule is zero for which of the following reasons?
options: The molecule contains only nonpolar bonds
The molecule contains polar bonds that are arranged symmetrically so their vector sum is zero
The molecule contains only one type of atom
The electronegativity difference between all atoms is exactly the same
explanation: Dipole moment = vector sum of all individual bond dipoles. For net خ¼ = 0, EITHER all bonds are nonpolar (خ”EN = 0) OR polar bonds cancel due to symmetry. Examples of symmetric cancellation: COâ‚‚ (2 C=O dipoles point in opposite directions, cancel â†’ خ¼ = 0), CClâ‚„ (4 C-Cl dipoles in tetrahedral arrangement, cancel), BFâ‚ƒ (3 B-F dipoles in trigonal planar, cancel). Hâ‚‚O and NHâ‚ƒ have polar bonds that do NOT cancel (asymmetric) â†’ خ¼ â‰  0. Knowing molecular geometry is essential to determining if dipoles cancel. Even a molecule of one atom type can have خ¼ = 0 trivially (Hâ‚‚, Clâ‚‚).

question: Which of the following best explains why HF has a higher boiling point (19.5آ°C) than HCl (âˆ’85آ°C), despite HF having a lower molecular mass?
options: HF has stronger London dispersion forces due to greater polarizability
HF molecules are associated through strong hydrogen bonding (F-Hآ·آ·آ·F), which requires significant energy to overcome
HF is a stronger acid and therefore more ionic
HF molecules pack more efficiently in liquid state
explanation: HF has a lower molar mass (20 g/mol) than HCl (36.5 g/mol), so London dispersion forces are weaker in HF. Yet HF boils at 19.5آ°C while HCl boils at âˆ’85آ°C â€” a 104.5آ°C difference. The explanation: F is the most electronegative element, and the H bonded to F is highly خ´+. This creates STRONG hydrogen bonds (F-Hآ·آ·آ·F) between HF molecules that must be broken during boiling. Each H-bond in HF has energy ~29 kJ/mol. HCl cannot form effective H-bonds (Cl is larger and less electronegative relative to its size). This same reasoning explains the anomalous boiling points of Hâ‚‚O and NHâ‚ƒ.

question: In which of the following does the central atom have the greatest number of lone pairs?
explanation: Count lone pairs on central atom: Hâ‚‚O: O has 4 valence electrons âˆ’ 2 shared in bonds = 4 nonbonding â†’ 2 lone pairs. NHâ‚ƒ: N has 5 âˆ’ 3 = 2 nonbonding â†’ 1 lone pair. XeFâ‚‚: Xe has 8 valence electrons âˆ’ 2 shared = 6 nonbonding â†’ 3 lone pairs (linear molecule, octahedral electron geometry with 3 equatorial lone pairs). PClâ‚ƒ: P has 5 âˆ’ 3 = 2 nonbonding â†’ 1 lone pair. XeFâ‚‚ has the most lone pairs (3) on the central atom, giving it the maximum VSEPR electron count of 5 (2 bp + 3 lp â†’ trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry, linear molecular geometry as lone pairs occupy equatorial positions).

question: According to VSEPR, the molecular geometry of SFâ‚† is:
explanation: SFâ‚†: S has 6 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs. 6 electron domains â†’ octahedral electron geometry AND octahedral molecular geometry (since all 6 domains are bonds). All F-S-F angles = 90آ°. Hybridization: spآ³dآ² (1s + 3p + 2d orbitals). SFâ‚† is an important industrial gas (electrical insulator), also a potent greenhouse gas. Octahedral geometry: 6 equivalent bond positions. Compare: PClâ‚… (5 bonds, 0 lp) = trigonal bipyramidal; BrFâ‚… (5 bonds, 1 lp) = square pyramidal; XeFâ‚„ (4 bonds, 2 lp) = square planar; IFâ‚‡ (7 bonds, 0 lp) = pentagonal bipyramidal.

question:
Assertion (A): The melting point of MgO (2852آ°C) is much higher than that of NaCl (801آ°C).
Reason (R): MgO has doubly charged ions (Mgآ²âپ؛ and Oآ²âپ») while NaCl has singly charged ions (Naâپ؛ and Clâپ»), resulting in much greater electrostatic attraction and lattice energy.
options: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true but R does not explain A
A is false, R is true
explanation: A is TRUE: MgO melts at 2852آ°C vs NaCl at 801آ°C â€” a massive difference. R is TRUE: Lattice energy âˆ‌ (qâ‚ٹ أ— qâ‚‹)/r. For NaCl: 1أ—1/r = 1/r. For MgO: 2أ—2/r' = 4/r'. Even accounting for similar ionic radii (Mgآ²âپ؛ â‰ˆ 72 pm, Oآ²âپ» â‰ˆ 140 pm, Naâپ؛ â‰ˆ 102 pm, Clâپ» â‰ˆ 181 pm â€” MgO has shorter interionic distance too), the 4أ— greater charge product dominates. MgO lattice energy â‰ˆ 3791 kJ/mol vs NaCl â‰ˆ 787 kJ/mol. Higher lattice energy â†’ higher melting point. R correctly explains A.

question: Which type of intermolecular force acts between all molecules, both polar and nonpolar?
explanation: London dispersion forces (van der Waals forces) exist between ALL molecules â€” polar and nonpolar â€” because they arise from temporary instantaneous dipoles caused by momentary, random electron fluctuations. Even nonpolar molecules like noble gases, Nâ‚‚, and CHâ‚„ have London forces, which is why they liquefy at low temperatures. London forces increase with: (1) molecular size (more electrons â†’ greater polarizability â†’ larger instantaneous dipoles), (2) molecular surface area (more contact = more interaction). Dipole-dipole forces exist only between POLAR molecules. H-bonding requires H bonded to F, O, or N. Ion-dipole forces require ions.

question: The bond order of Heâ‚‚ according to MO theory is zero. This means:
options: Heâ‚‚ is a stable molecule with very weak bonding
Heâ‚‚ does not exist as a stable molecule under normal conditions
Heâ‚‚ exists but only at very low temperatures
explanation: Heâ‚‚ MO configuration: دƒ1sآ² دƒ*1sآ². Bonding eâپ» = 2, antibonding eâپ» = 2. Bond order = (2âˆ’2)/2 = 0. BO = 0 means NO net bonding â€” Heâ‚‚ is unstable and does not exist as a stable molecule. This is why helium is monatomic. Similarly, Beâ‚‚ (دƒ1sآ² دƒ*1sآ² دƒ2sآ² دƒ*2sآ²) has BO = 0 and doesn't exist stably. MO theory thus explains why noble gases are monatomic: filling both bonding and antibonding MOs equally gives no net bonding. Note: Heâ‚‚âپ؛ (3 electrons, BO = 0.5) DOES exist momentarily but is very unstable.

question: The 'banana bonds' or bent bonds in cyclopropane (Câ‚ƒHâ‚†) explain its unusual chemical reactivity compared to other cycloalkanes. These bent bonds are characterized by:
options: Unusually strong C-C bonds due to high s-character
Poor orbital overlap because the bond orbitals cannot achieve perfect head-on overlap due to ring constraint (internal angle 60آ° vs spآ³ angle 109.5آ°)
Ionic character of C-C bonds in the ring
The presence of د€ bonds in the ring structure
explanation: In cyclopropane, the internal bond angles are forced to 60آ° (triangular geometry). But carbon is spآ³ hybridized with preferred angles of 109.5آ°. This creates 'angle strain' â€” the C-C bonding orbitals cannot achieve efficient head-on (axial) overlap at 60آ°; instead, they overlap at a bent angle (like a banana), with maximum overlap occurring outside the C-C axis. This bent bond model explains: (1) lower bond strength (weaker bonds due to poor overlap), (2) higher reactivity (cyclopropane undergoes ring-opening reactions like alkenes), (3) the 'ring strain' energy (~115 kJ/mol). Cyclopropane is used as an anesthetic.

question: NEET-pattern: Arrange the following in order of increasing bond angle: Hâ‚‚S, Hâ‚‚O, Hâ‚‚Se
explanation: Bond angles: Hâ‚‚O (104.5آ°) > Hâ‚‚S (92.1آ°) > Hâ‚‚Se (91آ°). Why? As we go down Group 16 (O â†’ S â†’ Se â†’ Te), the central atom becomes larger. Larger central atoms have more diffuse orbitals with less s-character in bonding â€” the bonds become more p-like (pure p-p bonds are at 90آ°). S and Se use nearly pure p orbitals for bonding (less hybridization tendency as you go down the group), giving angles approaching 90آ°. O's small size and high electronegativity promotes spآ³ hybridization (closer to 109.5آ°, but reduced by lone pairs to 104.5آ°). Order of increasing bond angle: Hâ‚‚Se (91آ°) < Hâ‚‚S (92.1آ°) < Hâ‚‚O (104.5آ°).

question: In the context of ionic bonding, the Born-Haber cycle is used to:
options: Calculate the boiling point of ionic compounds
Determine the standard enthalpy of formation of ionic compounds using Hess's law, with lattice energy as one component
Determine the rate of crystallization of ionic salts
Calculate the solubility product of ionic compounds
explanation: The Born-Haber cycle applies Hess's law to ionic compound formation, breaking the overall process into measurable steps: (1) Atomization of metal (sublimation energy), (2) Dissociation of non-metal (bond energy), (3) Ionization of metal (ionization energy), (4) Electron gain by non-metal (electron affinity), (5) Formation of crystal (lattice energy). The cycle allows calculation of the lattice energy (which cannot be directly measured) from other known thermochemical quantities. Example: for NaCl formation: خ”Hf = خ”Hsub(Na) + آ½D(Clâ‚‚) + IEâ‚پ(Na) + EA(Cl) + U(lattice). This is a key concept linking thermodynamics and bonding.

question: Which of the following exhibits ionic bonding with significant covalent character?
explanation: Covalent character in ionic bonds is explained by Fajans' rules: polarization (distortion of electron cloud of anion by cation) increases with: (1) high charge of cation, (2) small size of cation (high charge density), (3) large size of anion (easily polarized), (4) anion with 18-electron configuration. AlClâ‚ƒ: Alآ³âپ؛ has very high charge (+3) and small radius (53 pm) â†’ very high polarizing power â†’ significantly distorts Clâپ» electron cloud â†’ high covalent character. CsF: Csâپ؛ is large and has only +1 charge â†’ low polarizing power â†’ most ionic of all compounds. NaCl and KBr are typical ionic compounds. AlClâ‚ƒ is volatile, soluble in organic solvents â€” properties reflecting its high covalent character.

question: Benzene has a regular hexagonal structure with all C-C bond lengths equal (139 pm). This is best explained by:
options: Three alternating single and double bonds (Kekulأ© structures)
Resonance hybrid of two Kekulأ© structures resulting in delocalized د€ electrons across the ring
explanation: Kekulأ© structures (alternating C-C and C=C) would predict two different bond lengths (C-C: 154 pm, C=C: 134 pm), but all C-C bonds in benzene are equal at 139 pm (between single and double). This is explained by the resonance hybrid: benzene is a superposition of both Kekulأ© structures, with 6 د€ electrons delocalized over all 6 carbons in two ring-shaped molecular orbitals above and below the ring. The delocalization provides extra stability (resonance energy = 150 kJ/mol). All carbons are spآ² hybridized (trigonal planar, 120آ° angles). The uniform bond length of 139 pm is the experimental proof of resonance/delocalization.

question: The correct decreasing order of bond dissociation energy for diatomic molecules is:
explanation: Bond dissociation energies: Nâ‚‚ (945 kJ/mol, triple bond, BO=3) > Oâ‚‚ (498 kJ/mol, double bond, BO=2) > Fâ‚‚ (158 kJ/mol) > Clâ‚‚ (243 kJ/mol). Wait â€” Fâ‚‚ (158 kJ/mol) < Clâ‚‚ (243 kJ/mol)! This is an important anomaly. Fâ‚‚ has a weaker bond than Clâ‚‚ despite F being more electronegative, because: (1) F-F bond is short (142 pm) causing severe lone pair repulsion between the two small F atoms, (2) The 2p-2p overlap in Fâ‚‚ is less effective than 3p-3p in Clâ‚‚. So the order is Nâ‚‚ > Oâ‚‚ > Clâ‚‚ > Fâ‚‚. Option A says Nâ‚‚ > Oâ‚‚ > Fâ‚‚ > Clâ‚‚ which is wrong. Correct order is Nâ‚‚ > Oâ‚‚ > Clâ‚‚ > Fâ‚‚ â€” which is option B.

question: The geometry of SOâ‚„آ²âپ» ion is:
explanation: SOâ‚„آ²âپ» (sulfate): S has 6 valence electrons. In SOâ‚„آ²âپ», S forms 4 equivalent bonds (after including resonance/delocalization) with 4 oxygen atoms. 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs â†’ tetrahedral geometry (spآ³ hybridization). All O-S-O angles = 109.5آ°. S-O bond length in SOâ‚„آ²âپ» â‰ˆ 149 pm (shorter than a pure single bond due to partial double bond character from resonance). SOâ‚„آ²âپ» is isoelectronic and isostructural with POâ‚„آ³âپ», ClOâ‚„âپ», and CrOâ‚„آ²âپ» â€” all tetrahedral. The sulfate ion is the most common sulfur oxyanion and is present in gypsum (CaSOâ‚„آ·2Hâ‚‚O), widely used in construction.

question: CASE STUDY: A chemist is studying two compounds: Compound A (HF) and Compound B (HI). Both are hydrogen halides, but Compound A has a much higher boiling point (19.5آ°C) compared to Compound B (âˆ’35.4آ°C). The chemist measures the dipole moments: A = 1.91 D, B = 0.44 D. Based on this data, which explanation is most complete?
options: Compound A has a higher boiling point purely because of its greater dipole moment

Compound A forms strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds (F-Hآ·آ·آ·F) due to the high electronegativity and small size of F, whereas Compound B relies mainly on London dispersion forces and weak dipole interactions
Compound B has greater London forces due to its larger size, which should increase boiling point â€” the data is inconsistent
The boiling points are controlled by covalent bond strength, not intermolecular forces
explanation: Compound A (HF): the F-Hآ·آ·آ·F hydrogen bond is exceptionally strong (~29 kJ/mol) because F is the most electronegative and smallest element â€” the lone pair on F is in a compact, concentrated region. Despite HF's smaller size (lower London forces), its hydrogen bonding far dominates, raising its boiling point dramatically. Compound B (HI): I is large and polarizable (high London forces â€” explains why HI has higher bp than HBr which is higher than HCl among the other non-HF halides), but I is NOT electronegative enough to form effective H-bonds with H. So HI relies on weak dipole-dipole + London interactions. The 1.91D vs 0.44D dipole difference partially explains the difference, but the decisive factor is H-bonding in HF. This case perfectly illustrates the hierarchy of intermolecular forces.
